question:
5. Evaluate the following limits:
(i) \( \lim _{n \rightarrow \infty} \frac{n^{2}}{e^{n}} \).
(ii) \( \lim _{x \rightarrow 0} \frac{1}{x} \int_{0}^{x}(1+\sin 2 t)^{1 / t} d t \).
solution:
Solution. (i) It follows from L'Hospital's rule that
\[
\lim _{x \rightarrow \infty} \frac{x^{2}}{e^{x}}=\lim _{x \rightarrow \infty} \frac{2 x}{e^{x}}=\lim _{x \rightarrow \infty} \frac{2}{e^{x}}=0,
\]
whence the desired limit is zero.
Alternatively, one could use the fact that for \( x>0 \),
\[
e^{x}=\sum_{n=0}^{\infty} \frac{x^{n}}{n!}>\frac{x^{3}}{6}, \quad \text { whence } 0<\frac{x^{2}}{e^{x}}<\frac{6}{x}
\]
and, as \( x \rightarrow \infty, \lim _{x \rightarrow \infty} 6 / x=0 \), whence the desired limit is zero.
(ii) By L'Hospital's rule,
\[
\lim _{x \rightarrow 0} \frac{1}{x} \int_{0}^{x}(1+\sin 2 t)^{1 / t} d t=\lim _{x \rightarrow 0}(1+\sin 2 x)^{1 / x},
\]
provided the latter limit exists. Let \( \phi(x)=(1+\sin 2 x)^{1 / x} \). Then
\[
\log \phi(x)=\frac{\log (1+\sin 2 x)}{x} .
\]

Again using L'Hospital's rule,
\[
\lim _{x \rightarrow 0} \log \phi(x)=\lim _{x \rightarrow 0} \frac{2 \cos 2 x}{1+\sin 2 x}=2
\]

Since the exponential function is continuous,
\[
\lim _{x \rightarrow 0} \phi(x)=e^{2} .
\]

Therefore,
\[
\lim _{x \rightarrow 0} \frac{1}{x} \int_{0}^{x}(1+\sin 2 t)^{1 / \prime} d t=e^{2} .
\]

descriptive_long.question:
5. Evaluate the following limits:
(i) \( \lim _{indexvar \rightarrow \infty} \frac{indexvar^{2}}{e^{indexvar}} \).
(ii) \( \lim _{inputvar \rightarrow 0} \frac{1}{inputvar} \int_{0}^{inputvar}(1+\sin 2 timevar)^{1 / timevar} d timevar \).
descriptive_long.solution:
Solution. (i) It follows from L'Hospital's rule that
\[
\lim _{inputvar \rightarrow \infty} \frac{inputvar^{2}}{e^{inputvar}}=\lim _{inputvar \rightarrow \infty} \frac{2 inputvar}{e^{inputvar}}=\lim _{inputvar \rightarrow \infty} \frac{2}{e^{inputvar}}=0,
\]
whence the desired limit is zero.
Alternatively, one could use the fact that for \( inputvar>0 \),
\[
 e^{inputvar}=\sum_{indexvar=0}^{\infty} \frac{inputvar^{indexvar}}{indexvar!}>\frac{inputvar^{3}}{6}, \quad \text { whence } 0<\frac{inputvar^{2}}{e^{inputvar}}<\frac{6}{inputvar}
\]
and, as \( inputvar \rightarrow \infty, \lim _{inputvar \rightarrow \infty} 6 / inputvar=0 \), whence the desired limit is zero.
(ii) By L'Hospital's rule,
\[
\lim _{inputvar \rightarrow 0} \frac{1}{inputvar} \int_{0}^{inputvar}(1+\sin 2 timevar)^{1 / timevar} d timevar=\lim _{inputvar \rightarrow 0}(1+\sin 2 inputvar)^{1 / inputvar},
\]
provided the latter limit exists. Let \( phifunc(inputvar)=(1+\sin 2 inputvar)^{1 / inputvar} \). Then
\[
\log phifunc(inputvar)=\frac{\log (1+\sin 2 inputvar)}{inputvar} .
\]

Again using L'Hospital's rule,
\[
\lim _{inputvar \rightarrow 0} \log phifunc(inputvar)=\lim _{inputvar \rightarrow 0} \frac{2 \cos 2 inputvar}{1+\sin 2 inputvar}=2
\]

Since the exponential function is continuous,
\[
\lim _{inputvar \rightarrow 0} phifunc(inputvar)=e^{2} .
\]

Therefore,
\[
\lim _{inputvar \rightarrow 0} \frac{1}{inputvar} \int_{0}^{inputvar}(1+\sin 2 timevar)^{1 / \prime} d timevar=e^{2} .
\]

descriptive_long_confusing.question:
5. Evaluate the following limits:
(i) \( \lim _{daylighth \rightarrow \infty} \frac{daylighth^{2}}{e^{daylighth}} \).
(ii) \( \lim _{parakeets \rightarrow 0} \frac{1}{parakeets} \int_{0}^{parakeets}(1+\sin 2 snowflake)^{1 / snowflake} d snowflake \).
descriptive_long_confusing.solution:
Solution. (i) It follows from L'Hospital's rule that
\[
\lim _{parakeets \rightarrow \infty} \frac{parakeets^{2}}{e^{parakeets}}=\lim _{parakeets \rightarrow \infty} \frac{2 parakeets}{e^{parakeets}}=\lim _{parakeets \rightarrow \infty} \frac{2}{e^{parakeets}}=0,
\]
whence the desired limit is zero.
Alternatively, one could use the fact that for \( parakeets>0 \),
\[
 e^{parakeets}=\sum_{daylighth=0}^{\infty} \frac{parakeets^{daylighth}}{daylighth!}>\frac{parakeets^{3}}{6}, \quad \text { whence } 0<\frac{parakeets^{2}}{e^{parakeets}}<\frac{6}{parakeets}
\]
and, as \( parakeets \rightarrow \infty, \lim _{parakeets \rightarrow \infty} 6 / parakeets=0 \), whence the desired limit is zero.
(ii) By L'Hospital's rule,
\[
\lim _{parakeets \rightarrow 0} \frac{1}{parakeets} \int_{0}^{parakeets}(1+\sin 2 snowflake)^{1 / snowflake} d snowflake=\lim _{parakeets \rightarrow 0}(1+\sin 2 parakeets)^{1 / parakeets},
\]
provided the latter limit exists. Let \( starlight(parakeets)=(1+\sin 2 parakeets)^{1 / parakeets} \). Then
\[
\log starlight(parakeets)=\frac{\log (1+\sin 2 parakeets)}{parakeets} .
\]
Again using L'Hospital's rule,
\[
\lim _{parakeets \rightarrow 0} \log starlight(parakeets)=\lim _{parakeets \rightarrow 0} \frac{2 \cos 2 parakeets}{1+\sin 2 parakeets}=2
\]
Since the exponential function is continuous,
\[
\lim _{parakeets \rightarrow 0} starlight(parakeets)=e^{2} .
\]
Therefore,
\[
\lim _{parakeets \rightarrow 0} \frac{1}{parakeets} \int_{0}^{parakeets}(1+\sin 2 snowflake)^{1 / \prime} d snowflake=e^{2} .
\]

descriptive_long_misleading.question:
Problem:
<<<
5. Evaluate the following limits:
(i) \( \lim _{microscopic \rightarrow \infty} \frac{microscopic^{2}}{e^{microscopic}} \).
(ii) \( \lim _{fixedvalue \rightarrow 0} \frac{1}{fixedvalue} \int_{0}^{fixedvalue}(1+\sin 2 steadytime)^{1 / steadytime} d steadytime \).
>>>
descriptive_long_misleading.solution:
Solution:
<<<
Solution. (i) It follows from L'Hospital's rule that
\[
\lim _{fixedvalue \rightarrow \infty} \frac{fixedvalue^{2}}{e^{fixedvalue}}=\lim _{fixedvalue \rightarrow \infty} \frac{2 fixedvalue}{e^{fixedvalue}}=\lim _{fixedvalue \rightarrow \infty} \frac{2}{e^{fixedvalue}}=0,
\]
whence the desired limit is zero.
Alternatively, one could use the fact that for \( fixedvalue>0 \),
\[
 e^{fixedvalue}=\sum_{microscopic=0}^{\infty} \frac{fixedvalue^{microscopic}}{microscopic!}>\frac{fixedvalue^{3}}{6}, \quad \text { whence } 0<\frac{fixedvalue^{2}}{e^{fixedvalue}}<\frac{6}{fixedvalue}
\]
and, as \( fixedvalue \rightarrow \infty, \lim _{fixedvalue \rightarrow \infty} 6 / fixedvalue=0 \), whence the desired limit is zero.
(ii) By L'Hospital's rule,
\[
\lim _{fixedvalue \rightarrow 0} \frac{1}{fixedvalue} \int_{0}^{fixedvalue}(1+\sin 2 steadytime)^{1 / steadytime} d steadytime=\lim _{fixedvalue \rightarrow 0}(1+\sin 2 fixedvalue)^{1 / fixedvalue},
\]
provided the latter limit exists. Let \( \nonvaryfunc(fixedvalue)=(1+\sin 2 fixedvalue)^{1 / fixedvalue} \). Then
\[
\log \nonvaryfunc(fixedvalue)=\frac{\log (1+\sin 2 fixedvalue)}{fixedvalue} .
\]

Again using L'Hospital's rule,
\[
\lim _{fixedvalue \rightarrow 0} \log \nonvaryfunc(fixedvalue)=\lim _{fixedvalue \rightarrow 0} \frac{2 \cos 2 fixedvalue}{1+\sin 2 fixedvalue}=2
\]

Since the exponential function is continuous,
\[
\lim _{fixedvalue \rightarrow 0} \nonvaryfunc(fixedvalue)=e^{2} .
\]

Therefore,
\[
\lim _{fixedvalue \rightarrow 0} \frac{1}{fixedvalue} \int_{0}^{fixedvalue}(1+\sin 2 steadytime)^{1 / \prime} d steadytime=e^{2} .
\]
>>>

garbled_string.question:
5. Evaluate the following limits:
(i) \( \lim _{qzxwvtnp \rightarrow \infty} \frac{qzxwvtnp^{2}}{e^{qzxwvtnp}} \).
(ii) \( \lim _{hjgmrslk \rightarrow 0} \frac{1}{hjgmrslk} \int_{0}^{hjgmrslk}(1+\sin 2 lkjhadfg)^{1 / lkjhadfg} d lkjhadfg \).
garbled_string.solution:
Solution. (i) It follows from L'Hospital's rule that
\[
\lim _{hjgmrslk \rightarrow \infty} \frac{hjgmrslk^{2}}{e^{hjgmrslk}}=\lim _{hjgmrslk \rightarrow \infty} \frac{2 hjgmrslk}{e^{hjgmrslk}}=\lim _{hjgmrslk \rightarrow \infty} \frac{2}{e^{hjgmrslk}}=0,
\]
whence the desired limit is zero.
Alternatively, one could use the fact that for \( hjgmrslk>0 \),
\[
e^{hjgmrslk}=\sum_{qzxwvtnp=0}^{\infty} \frac{hjgmrslk^{qzxwvtnp}}{qzxwvtnp!}>\frac{hjgmrslk^{3}}{6}, \quad \text { whence } 0<\frac{hjgmrslk^{2}}{e^{hjgmrslk}}<\frac{6}{hjgmrslk}
\]
and, as \( hjgmrslk \rightarrow \infty, \lim _{hjgmrslk \rightarrow \infty} 6 / hjgmrslk=0 \), whence the desired limit is zero.
(ii) By L'Hospital's rule,
\[
\lim _{hjgmrslk \rightarrow 0} \frac{1}{hjgmrslk} \int_{0}^{hjgmrslk}(1+\sin 2 lkjhadfg)^{1 / lkjhadfg} d lkjhadfg=\lim _{hjgmrslk \rightarrow 0}(1+\sin 2 hjgmrslk)^{1 / hjgmrslk},
\]
provided the latter limit exists. Let \( ervmndol(hjgmrslk)=(1+\sin 2 hjgmrslk)^{1 / hjgmrslk} \). Then
\[
\log ervmndol(hjgmrslk)=\frac{\log (1+\sin 2 hjgmrslk)}{hjgmrslk} .
\]

Again using L'Hospital's rule,
\[
\lim _{hjgmrslk \rightarrow 0} \log ervmndol(hjgmrslk)=\lim _{hjgmrslk \rightarrow 0} \frac{2 \cos 2 hjgmrslk}{1+\sin 2 hjgmrslk}=2
\]

Since the exponential function is continuous,
\[
\lim _{hjgmrslk \rightarrow 0} ervmndol(hjgmrslk)=e^{2} .
\]

Therefore,
\[
\lim _{hjgmrslk \rightarrow 0} \frac{1}{hjgmrslk} \int_{0}^{hjgmrslk}(1+\sin 2 lkjhadfg)^{1 / \prime} d lkjhadfg=e^{2} .
\]

kernel_variant.question:
5. Evaluate the limits:

(i) \(\displaystyle \lim_{n\to\infty}\frac{n^{5}}{3^{n}}.\)

(ii) \(\displaystyle \lim_{x\to 0}\;\frac{1}{x}\int_{0}^{x}\left(\frac{2+\sin 7t}{2}\right)^{\!\frac{3}{t}}\,dt.\)
kernel_variant.solution:
Corrected solution

(i) Let f(x)=x^{5}/3^{x}, x>0. Apply L'Hopital's rule repeatedly:
    lim_{x\to \infty } x^{5}/3^{x} = lim_{x\to \infty } (5x^{4})/(3^{x}ln3)
                          = lim_{x\to \infty } (20x^{3})/(3^{x}(ln3)^{2})
                          = \ldots  = lim_{x\to \infty } (5!)/((ln3)^{5}3^{x}) = 0.
Hence  lim_{n\to \infty } n^{5}/3^{n} = 0.

(ii) Define F(t)=((2+sin7t)/2)^{3/t} for t\neq 0.  First find L=lim_{t\to 0}F(t).  Take logarithms:
    ln F(t)= (3/t)[ln(2+sin7t)-ln2].
This is a 0/0 form, so by L'Hopital's rule,
    lim_{t\to 0} ln F(t)= 3\cdot lim_{t\to 0} (7cos7t)/(2+sin7t) = 3\cdot (7/2) = 21/2.
Hence L = e^{21/2}.

Now set G(x)=(1/x)\int _{0}^{x}F(t)
    dt.  Since F(t)\to L as t\to 0, the average value G(x)\to L as x\to 0.  Equivalently, by L'Hopital's rule,
    lim_{x\to 0}G(x)= lim_{x\to 0}F(x)= e^{21/2}.

Final answers
(i) 0
(ii) e^{21/2}

core_steps: Use that an exponential grows faster than any fixed-degree polynomial (e.g. via repeated L’Hospital).
Convert the average-value limit  (1/x)∫₀ˣ F(t)dt  into  lim_{x→0} F(x)  by L’Hospital.
Take logarithms so a power of the form F(x)^{1/x} becomes a quotient  ln F(x)/x.
Apply L’Hospital to this 0/0 quotient to obtain a finite constant C = F'(0)/F(0).
Exponentiate to get the final limit e^{C}.

slot1.description: Degree of the polynomial in the first limit

slot2.description: Base of the exponential in the first limit (any constant >1 works)

slot3.description: Coefficient inside the sine argument, i.e. sin(k t)

slot4.description: Additive constant that makes the base near 1, i.e. A + sin(k t)

slot5.description: Numerator of the exponent in F(t)^{c/t}
